stories.headline: Avalanche's Central Division lead shrinks after 5-2 loss to Minnesota Wild
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche dropped a 5-2 decision to the Minnesota Wild on Thursday night at Ball Arena, allowing Minnesota to close to within five points in the Central Division standings, though Colorado holds two games in hand. Special teams were the central issue, as the Avalanche took six penalties and went 0-for-3 on the power play, while the Wild converted two of six power-play opportunities, both in the second period.

Joel Eriksson Ek scored twice on the power play for Minnesota, including a fortuitous goal late in the second period that resulted from a sequence of three Colorado penalties in 53 seconds — including an extended 5-on-3 situation following a delay-of-game call on Brent Burns. Goaltender Mackenzie Blackwood made 31 saves in his first start since the Olympic break, while Filip Gustavsson stopped 44 shots for the visitors.

Coach Jared Bednar said the penalty trouble was unacceptable against a team with Minnesota's power-play capability. The Avalanche have now gone without a power-play goal in consecutive games and are converting at just 15.1% with the man advantage since Jan. 16, last in the NHL. Colorado's ability to maintain its division lead will depend on correcting those special teams struggles over the stretch run of the regular season.

stories.headline: Sierra Space names Dan Jablonsky as CEO after yearlong search
stories.summary:
Sierra Space, the Louisville-based commercial space company, has appointed Dan Jablonsky as its new chief executive officer following a search that lasted more than a year, the Denver Business Journal reported Thursday. Jablonsky previously served as CEO of Maxar Technologies and has also held a leadership role at rocket propulsion startup Ursa Major, giving him deep roots in Colorado's aerospace sector.

Sierra Space is developing the Dream Chaser spaceplane under a NASA contract and has been working to establish itself as a major player in commercial space infrastructure. The company's search for permanent leadership had been closely watched in the local aerospace community, which has grown substantially in the Denver metro area in recent years.

The appointment comes at a pivotal time for Sierra Space as it works toward the first orbital flight of Dream Chaser and pursues additional government and commercial contracts. Jablonsky's familiarity with both satellite systems and emerging propulsion technologies is expected to be an asset as the company navigates a competitive landscape that includes SpaceX and other well-funded rivals.

stories.headline: Auto shop operator seeks community support after Thornton grass fire destroys roughly 100 vehicles
stories.summary:
Jason Rol, the operator of Rol Race Motorsports on the 480 block of West 84th Avenue in Thornton, launched a GoFundMe fundraiser after Wednesday's grass fire swept through a field behind his shop and destroyed approximately 100 vehicles belonging to him and his customers. Rol said many of the cars lost had been left in his care by clients and called the aftermath heartbreaking for himself, his customers and surrounding neighbors.

The fire ignited in a grassy area east of Pinnacle High School and spread rapidly under dry conditions and high winds, sending a thick column of black smoke visible for miles across the Denver metro area. Four emergency responders and one other person were injured, none critically, and crews achieved substantial containment within about three hours of the initial report.

Within 21 hours of its creation, the fundraiser had collected more than $3,400 — over half of its $6,000 goal — from community donors. Rol wrote on the fundraiser page that he feels a deep responsibility to support those who trusted him with their vehicles and personal belongings as they work to rebuild.

stories.headline: Colorado convenes drought task force as dry winter raises alarm over summer water and wildfire risk
stories.summary:
Colorado officials have begun organizing a multiagency drought task force in response to what experts describe as a historically dry and warm winter and a spring forecast offering little relief, the Colorado Sun reported Thursday. The task force will draw from agencies overseeing water, agriculture and emergency management, and could coordinate with the governor's office to release state emergency funds if conditions worsen, according to Emily Adrid of the Colorado Water Conservation Board.

State climatologist Russ Schumacher said the first four months of the 2026-27 water year are the warmest in 131 years, breaking the previous record by a wide margin. Statewide reservoir storage remains above 80%, but streamflow forecasts from the Natural Resources Conservation Service in Lakewood project Colorado will receive only about 63% of normal water flows — and possibly less — through the summer months.

Cities across the metro area are already gearing up for potential watering restrictions and public outreach campaigns. Westminster water resources manager Drew Beckwith said the greater concern is not a single dry year but successive drought years, noting the state has not faced a major drought since 2002 and that agencies need to coordinate messaging and planning now. Officials said even significant spring snowstorms would be unlikely to fully reverse the water deficit heading into summer.
